---
TITLE: Data Preparation – Wholesale & Retail Credit Risk Inputs
AUTHOR: Credit Risk Modeler
DATE: 2025-04-08
VERSION: 1.0
---

## 1. DATA SOURCES

| Source             | Table / File          | Coverage Period | Records | Key Fields                         |
|--------------------|-----------------------|-----------------|---------|------------------------------------|
| Core Banking Sys   | LOAN_MASTER           | 2018Q1–2025Q1   | 85,432  | loan_id, obligor_id, orig_date, maturity, product_type, exposure_amt, currency |
| Credit Risk Warehouse | PD_INPUTS          | 2018Q1–2025Q1   | 85,432  | loan_id, PD_12mo, rating, rating_agency, financial_covenants |
| Loss Given Default  | LGD_INPUTS           | 2018Q1–2025Q1   | 12,710  | loan_id, collateral_type, collateral_value, seniority, recovery_rate, workout_cost |
| Exposure at Default | EAD_INPUTS           | 2018Q1–2025Q1   | 85,432  | loan_id, drawn_amt, undrawn_amt, CCF_applied, EAD_estimate |
| Default Flag        | DEFAULT_FLAGS        | 2018Q1–2025Q1   | 85,432  | loan_id, default_date, default_flag (0/1), cure_flag (0/1), cure_date |
| Macro/Forecast      | MACRO_DATA           | 2018Q1–2025Q1   | 29      | quarter, GDP_growth, unemployment_rate, HPI, PMI, industry_risk_index |

## 2. RECONCILIATION LOG

### 2.1 Record Count Reconciliation
| Step                          | Count Before | Removed | Matched | Count After |
|-------------------------------|-------------|---------|---------|------------|
| Raw LOAN_MASTER               | 85,432      | —       | —       | 85,432     |
| Merge with PD_INPUTS          | 85,432      | 214     | 85,218  | 85,218     |
| Merge with LGD_INPUTS (defaults only) | 12,710 | 103     | 12,607  | 12,607     |
| Merge with EAD_INPUTS         | 85,218      | 89      | 85,129  | 85,129     |
| Merge with DEFAULT_FLAGS      | 85,129      | 0       | 85,129  | 85,129     |
| Merge with MACRO_DATA         | 85,129      | 0       | 85,129  | 85,129     |

### 2.2 Missing Data Treatment
| Field                | Missing % | Treatment Applied                                        |
|----------------------|-----------|--------------------------------------------------------|
| collateral_value     | 4.2%      | Imputed with median by product_type × seniority         |
| recovery_rate        | 2.8%      | Forward-filled from last observed within same sector    |
| workout_cost         | 5.1%      | Set to 2.5% of EAD if missing (industry benchmark)      |
| CCF_applied          | 1.3%      | Use product-type average CCF (0.45 for revolver, 1.0 for term loan) |
| rating               | 0.7%      | Mapped from internal scorecard; 0.3% remained unmapped → flagged |
| cure_flag            | 0.0%      | Fully populated from default tracking system            |

### 2.3 Outlier Detection
- **Exposure > 99.9th percentile**: 85 loans capped at 99.9th percentile value.
- **PD > 0.50 (50%)**: 23 obligors reviewed; 18 confirmed as distressed, 5 data errors corrected.
- **Recovery rate > 1.0**: 7 records truncated to 1.0 (non-binding data entry).

## 3. FINAL RECONCILED DATASET SUMMARY

| Metric                    | Value         |
|---------------------------|--------------|
| Total Loans               | 85,129       |
| Unique Obligors           | 62,418       |
| Defaulted Loans (history) | 12,607       |
| Default Rate (avg)        | 3.2%         |
| Cured Loans               | 2,891         |
| Retail Portfolio Share    | 58%          |
| Wholesale Portfolio Share | 42%          |
| Observation Periods       | 29 quarters   |

name: Defaults_Cures_Downturn_Adjustments.md
description: Treatment framework for default definition, cure logic, and downturn adjustments across PD/LGD/EAD
content:
---
TITLE: Defaults, Cures & Downturn Adjustments – Treatment Framework
VERSION: 1.0
---

## 1. DEFAULT DEFINITION (Basel II / IFRS 9 Aligned)

**Criteria** (any of the following triggers default):
1. Days past due > 90 days on any material credit obligation.
2. Obligor is classified as 'unlikely to pay' (UTP) by credit risk team.
3. Bankruptcy filing, restructuring, or debt forgiveness.
4. Regulatory classification as non-performing (NPL) for > 90 days.

**Look-back period**: 12 months from observation date for default flag.

## 2. CURE TREATMENT

**Cure Definition**:
A loan is considered 'cured' if it meets ALL of the following:
- Defaulted at some point in history
- Returned to performing status (no default flag) for ≥ 6 consecutive months
- All arrears have been cleared
- No new forbearance or restructuring in the cure period

**Cure Logic in Dataset**:
| Variable        | Description                                              |
|-----------------|----------------------------------------------------------|
| default_flag    | 1 = currently in default; 0 = performing (incl. cured)    |
| ever_defaulted  | 1 = has defaulted at any point in history; 0 = never      |
| cure_flag       | 1 = has defaulted AND subsequently cured per criteria; 0 = otherwise |
| cure_date       | Date when cure criteria were first satisfied              |
| cured_ever      | 1 = ever met cure criteria; 0 = never cured               |

**Treatment in Modeling**:
- **PD modeling**: Cured loans are included in the 'non-default' population AFTER the cure date. Prior to cure, they are treated as defaulted.
- **LGD modeling**: Cured loans have their own LGD (typically lower than non-cured defaults). Separate LGD parameters estimated for 'cured' vs 'non-cured' defaulted loans.
- **EAD modeling**: Cured loans may retain a higher CCF for 12 months post-cure (conservative flagging).

## 3. DOWNTURN ADJUSTMENTS

### 3.1 Downturn Identification
Using historical macro data (2018Q1–2025Q1), downturn periods are identified as:

| Downturn Period   | Key Triggering Event                          | GDP Growth | Unemployment |
|-------------------|-----------------------------------------------|------------|-------------|
| 2020Q1–2020Q3     | COVID-19 pandemic initial shock               | -8.4%      | 14.8% (peak)|
| 2022Q2–2022Q4     | Inflation surge & rate hikes                  | -0.6%      | 3.6%        |

### 3.2 Downturn LGD
**Methodology**: Long-run default-weighted average LGD during downturn periods.

| Segment               | Normal LGD | Downturn LGD | Downturn Multiplier |
|-----------------------|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| Wholesale – Secured   | 38.2%     | 47.5%       | 1.24×               |
| Wholesale – Unsecured | 62.5%     | 74.1%       | 1.19×               |
| Retail – Mortgage     | 22.3%     | 30.1%       | 1.35×               |
| Retail – Credit Card  | 45.8%     | 58.2%       | 1.27×               |
| Retail – Other        | 40.1%     | 52.4%       | 1.31×               |

**Downturn LGD Formula**:
```
LGD_downturn = Σ (LGD_i × 1_{downturn}) / Σ (1_{downturn})
```

### 3.3 Downturn PD (PIT vs TTC)
- **Point-in-Time (PIT) PD**: Used for IFRS 9 Stage 1/2, reflects current macro conditions.
  - `PIT_PD = baseline_PD × exp(β × Δmacro)`
- **Through-the-Cycle (TTC) PD**: Used for Basel capital, smoothes over cycle.
  - `TTC_PD = long-run average PD ≈ 3.2%`
- **Downturn PD (stress)**: Used for stress testing / economic capital.
  - `Downturn_PD = max(TTC_PD × 1.5, PIT_PD under downturn macro)`

### 3.4 Downturn CCF (Credit Conversion Factor)
| Product             | Normal CCF | Downturn CCF |
|---------------------|-----------|-------------|
| Revolver (Retail)   | 0.45      | 0.65        |
| Revolver (Wholesale)| 0.50      | 0.70        |
| Term Loan           | 1.00      | 1.00        |
| Overdraft           | 0.75      | 0.85        |
| Credit Card         | 0.40      | 0.60        |

## 4. FINAL OUTPUT VARIABLES

| Variable             | Type    | Description                                           |
|----------------------|---------|-------------------------------------------------------|
| loan_id              | str     | Unique loan identifier                                |
| obligor_id           | str     | Unique obligor identifier                             |
| product_type         | cat     | {'Term Loan', 'Revolver', 'Mortgage', 'Credit Card', 'Overdraft'} |
| segment              | cat     | {'Wholesale', 'Retail'}                               |
| rating               | int     | Internal rating grade (1=best … 10=worst)             |
| PD_12mo              | float   | 12-month probability of default (PIT)                 |
| PD_TTC               | float   | Through-the-cycle PD                                  |
| PD_downturn          | float   | Downturn-stressed PD                                  |
| LGD_normal           | float   | Long-run average LGD (non-downturn)                   |
| LGD_downturn         | float   | Downturn-adjusted LGD                                 |
| EAD                  | float   | Exposure at default (drawn + CCF × undrawn)           |
| EAD_downturn         | float   | Downturn-adjusted EAD (using downturn CCF)            |
| default_flag         | bool    | 1 = defaulted at observation date                     |
| ever_defaulted       | bool    | 1 = ever defaulted in history                         |
| cure_flag            | bool    | 1 = cured per defined criteria                        |
| collateral_value     | float   | Collateral value (if any)                             |
| seniority            | cat     | {'Senior Secured', 'Senior Unsecured', 'Subordinated'}|

name: ICAAP_Architecture_Document_v1.0.md
description: Comprehensive ICAAP (Internal Capital Adequacy Assessment Process) Architecture Document covering the full architecture, integration with business planning, governance framework, data architecture, systems, and implementation roadmap. Includes RACI matrix, committee structure, three lines of defense, and capital planning cycle.
content:
# ICAAP Architecture Document

**Document Title:** Internal Capital Adequacy Assessment Process (ICAAP) – Architecture & Integration with Planning and Governance

**Version:** 1.0
**Date:** 2025-04-17
**Status:** Final Draft
**Document Owner:** Chief Risk Officer (CRO)

---

## 1. Executive Summary

This document defines the architecture of the Internal Capital Adequacy Assessment Process (ICAAP) and describes how it integrates with the institution's planning and governance frameworks. The ICAAP is a forward-looking, risk-based process that ensures the institution:

- Maintains capital levels commensurate with its risk profile
- Identifies, measures, and manages all material risks
- Integrates capital planning with strategic business planning
- Operates under a robust governance and oversight framework

The architecture is designed to comply with regulatory requirements (e.g., Basel Pillar 2, CRD IV/V, EBA Guidelines on ICAAP, PRA SS31/15, etc.) and to embed a strong risk culture throughout the organization.

---

## 2. Scope and Applicability

This document applies to:

- All legal entities within the group (on a solo and consolidated basis)
- All material risk types (credit, market, operational, liquidity, concentration, business, strategic, reputational, and emerging risks)
- All business lines and support functions
- The capital planning cycle (minimum annually, with quarterly reviews and ad-hoc triggers)

---

## 3. Architectural Principles

The ICAAP architecture is built on the following principles:

| # | Principle | Description |
|---|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Risk-Based** | Capital adequacy is assessed against the actual risk profile, not just regulatory minima |
| 2 | **Forward-Looking** | The process incorporates stress testing and scenario analysis to anticipate future risks |
| 3 | **Proportionality** | The depth and sophistication of the approach is commensurate with the size, complexity, and risk profile of the institution |
| 4 | **Integrated** | ICAAP is embedded into strategic planning, budgeting, and risk appetite setting |
| 5 | **Comprehensive** | All material risks are captured, including those not covered by Pillar 1 |
| 6 | **Governed** | Clear ownership, accountability, and independent oversight exist at all levels |
| 7 | **Documented** | All methodologies, assumptions, results, and decisions are formally documented and auditable |

---

## 4. ICAAP Architecture Overview

### 4.1 High-Level Architecture Diagram (Conceptual)

```
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|                    GOVERNANCE LAYER                          |
|  Board / Board Risk Committee / CRO / Internal Audit        |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                              |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|                    ICAAP CORE COMPONENTS                     |
|                                                              |
|  +------------------+  +------------------+  +------------+ |
|  | Risk ID &        |  | Risk Measurement |  | Capital    | |
|  | Materiality      |  | & Quantification |  | Assessment | |
|  +------------------+  +------------------+  +------------+ |
|                                                              |
|  +------------------+  +------------------+  +------------+ |
|  | Stress Testing   |  | Capital Planning |  | ORSA /     | |
|  | & Scenarios      |  | & Allocation     |  | RAF Link   | |
|  +------------------+  +------------------+  +------------+ |
|                                                              |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                              |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|                    DATA & SYSTEMS LAYER                      |
|  Risk Data Warehouse / ALM System / Trading Systems / GL    |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|                                                              |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
|                    INPUT LAYER                               |
|  Business Plans / Budgets / Economic Forecasts / Reg Data   |
+-------------------------------------------------------------+
```

### 4.2 Core Components

#### 4.2.1 Risk Identification and Materiality Assessment
- **Purpose:** Identify all current and emerging risks that could materially impact capital adequacy
- **Frequency:** Annual (full review) with quarterly updates and ad-hoc triggers
- **Output:** Risk Register, Risk Taxonomy, Materiality Matrix
- **Methodology:** Top-down and bottom-up risk identification workshops, expert judgment, industry peer analysis

#### 4.2.2 Risk Measurement and Quantification
- **Purpose:** Quantify risks in capital terms using appropriate methodologies
- **Components:**
  - Credit Risk: IRB/SA, ECL models, concentration risk add-ons
  - Market Risk: VaR, SVaR, IRC, stress scenarios
  - Operational Risk: AMA, standardized approach, scenario analysis
  - Liquidity Risk: LCR, NSFR, liquidity stress tests
  - Business Risk: Earnings at Risk, economic capital models
  - Concentration Risk: Herfindahl-Hirschman Index, single-name limits
  - Emerging Risks: Qualitative assessment, scenario planning

#### 4.2.3 Capital Assessment
- **Purpose:** Determine the level and composition of capital required to cover risks
- **Output:** Internal Capital Target (ICT), Capital Buffer, Capital Conservation Plan
- **Methodology:** Economic capital models, regulatory capital calculations, hybrid approaches

#### 4.2.4 Stress Testing and Scenario Analysis
- **Purpose:** Assess capital adequacy under adverse conditions
- **Types:**
  - Baseline scenario (business-as-usual)
  - Adverse scenario (severe but plausible)
  - Severely adverse scenario (tail risk)
  - Reverse stress testing (identify failure points)
  - Sensitivity analysis
- **Frequency:** Annual with quarterly updates

#### 4.2.5 Capital Planning and Allocation
- **Purpose:** Ensure capital is available to support strategic objectives and risk appetite
- **Output:** Capital Plan, Capital Budget, Allocation Framework
- **Linkage:** Tied to business planning cycle, strategic initiatives, and dividend policy

#### 4.2.6 Risk Appetite Framework (RAF) Linkage
- **Purpose:** Ensure ICAAP is consistent with the institution's risk appetite
- **Output:** Risk Appetite Statement (RAS), Risk Limits, Risk Triggers
- **Integration:** Capital targets are set within risk appetite boundaries; stress test results inform risk appetite calibration

---

## 5. Integration with Planning

### 5.1 Capital Planning Cycle

The ICAAP is deeply integrated with the annual business planning and budgeting cycle:

```
+------------------------------------------------------------+
|  Strategic Planning (Q1)                                   |
|  - Define 3-5 year strategic objectives                    |
|  - Set risk appetite                                      |
|  - Identify key business initiatives                      |
+------------------------------------------------------------+
               |
               v
+------------------------------------------------------------+
|  Business Planning & Budgeting (Q2)                        |
|  - Revenue projections by business line                   |
|  - Cost forecasts                                          |
|  - Balance sheet projections                               |
|  - Capital expenditure plans                               |
+------------------------------------------------------------+
               |
               v
+------------------------------------------------------------+
|  ICAAP Risk Assessment (Q2-Q3)                             |
|  - Risk identification & materiality                       |
|  - Risk quantification                                     |
|  - Stress testing & scenario analysis                     |
|  - Capital adequacy assessment                             |
+------------------------------------------------------------+
               |
               v
+------------------------------------------------------------+
|  Capital Planning (Q3)                                     |
|  - Set internal capital targets                            |
|  - Capital allocation to business lines                    |
|  - Capital contingency planning                            |
+------------------------------------------------------------+
               |
               v
+------------------------------------------------------------+
|  Board Approval & Regulatory Submission (Q4)               |
|  - ICAAP report review and challenge                      |
|  - Board approval                                          |
|  - Regulatory submission (Pillar 3, SREP, etc.)           |
+------------------------------------------------------------+
               |
               v
+------------------------------------------------------------+
|  Monitoring & Reporting (Ongoing)                          |
|  - Quarterly capital monitoring                            |
|  - Trigger/adverse event reviews                           |
|  - Management reporting                                    |
+------------------------------------------------------------+
```

### 5.2 Key Integration Points

| Planning Component | ICAAP Integration | Output |
|--------------------|-------------------|--------|
| Strategic Plan | Risk appetite calibration, capital adequacy constraints | Maximum risk capacity, capital targets |
| Business Plan | Revenue/balance sheet projections feed stress scenarios | Stress-test-adjusted P&L |
| Budget | Capital allocation decisions, dividend policy | Capital budget, dividend limits |
| M&A / Growth Plans | Capital impact assessment, integration risk | Capital add-ons, buffer requirements |
| Recovery Plan | Reverse stress testing, capital triggers | Recovery triggers, capital restoration options |

### 5.3 Data Flow Between Planning and ICAAP

```
Planning System (e.g., Anaplan, Hyperion)
  |
  |-- Business plan assumptions (revenue, costs, volumes) --> ICAAP Engine
  |-- Balance sheet projections --> ICAAP Engine
  |-- Strategic initiatives --> ICAAP Engine
  |
  v
ICAAP Engine
  |
  |-- Risk-adjusted capital targets --> Planning System
  |-- Stress test results --> Planning System (for contingency planning)
  |-- Capital allocation constraints --> Planning System
  |-- Risk appetite limits --> Planning System
  |
  v
Reporting & Dashboards
```

---

## 6. Integration with Governance

### 6.1 Three Lines of Defense Model

| Line of Defense | Role in ICAAP | Responsibilities |
|-----------------|---------------|-----------------|
| **1st Line** (Business Lines) | Risk ownership | - Identify and manage risks in daily operations |
| | | - Provide input to risk identification and scenario design |
| | | - Implement risk mitigation actions |
| | | - Escalate breaches of risk appetite |
| **2nd Line** (Risk Management, Compliance) | ICAAP oversight | - Design and maintain ICAAP framework |
| | | - Challenge 1st line risk assessments |
| | | - Conduct independent risk quantification |
| | | - Prepare ICAAP report |
| | | - Monitor compliance with risk limits |
| **3rd Line** (Internal Audit) | Independent assurance | - Audit ICAAP process and controls |
| | | - Validate methodologies and assumptions |
| | | - Report findings to Board Audit Committee |

### 6.2 Committee Structure and Responsibilities

```
                              +----------------------------+
                              |      Board of Directors     |
                              | - Approves ICAAP policy    |
                              | - Sets risk appetite       |
                              | - Approves capital targets |
                              +----------------------------+
                                         |
                   +---------------------+---------------------+
                   |                                           |
                   v                                           v
    +---------------------------+           +----------------------------+
    | Board Risk Committee (BRC)|           | Board Audit Committee (BAC)|
    | - Reviews ICAAP results   |           | - Oversees internal audit  |
    | - Challenges assumptions  |           |   of ICAAP                 |
    | - Recommends to Board     |           | - Reviews findings         |
    +---------------------------+           +----------------------------+
                   |
                   v
    +---------------------------+
    | Asset & Liability Comm.   |
    | (ALCO)                    |
    | - Reviews capital adequacy|
    | - Manages capital actions |
    | - Monitors limits         |
    +---------------------------+
                   |
                   v
    +---------------------------+
    | Management Risk Committee |
    | (MRC)                     |
    | - Operational oversight   |
    | - Reviews stress tests    |
    | - Escalates issues        |
    +---------------------------+
                   |
                   v
    +---------------------------+
    | Capital Working Group     |
    | - Technical ICAAP work    |
    | - Model development       |
    | - Data preparation        |
    +---------------------------+
```

### 6.3 RACI Matrix for ICAAP Activities

| Activity | Board | BRC | CRO | CFO | Risk Mgmt | Finance | Bus. Lines | Internal Audit |
|----------|-------|-----|-----|-----|-----------|---------|------------|----------------|
| ICAAP Policy Approval | **A** | R | C | C | C | - | - | - |
| Risk Appetite Setting | **A** | R | C | C | C | C | C | - |
| Risk Identification | - | - | R | - | **A** | C | C | - |
| Risk Quantification | - | - | - | - | **A** | C | C | - |
| Stress Testing | - | - | C | C | **A** | R | C | - |
| Capital Target Setting | A | **R** | C | C | C | C | - | - |
| ICAAP Report Review | I | **A** | R | C | C | C | - | - |
| ICAAP Audit | - | - | I | I | I | I | - | **A** |

*R = Responsible, A = Accountable, C = Consulted, I = Informed*

### 6.4 Governance Documentation

| Document | Owner | Frequency | Contents |
|----------|-------|-----------|----------|
| ICAAP Policy | CRO | Annual | Overall framework, principles, roles |
| Risk Appetite Statement | Board | Annual | Quantitative and qualitative risk appetite |
| ICAAP Methodology Document | Risk Mgmt | Annual | Detailed methodologies, models, assumptions |
| ICAAP Report | CRO | Annual | Assessment results, conclusions, recommendations |
| Capital Plan | CFO | Annual | Capital targets, sources, uses, contingency |
| Stress Testing Report | Risk Mgmt | Quarterly | Scenario results, impact analysis |
| Risk Register | Risk Mgmt | Quarterly | Risk inventory, materiality, trends |
| Audit Report | Internal Audit | Annual | Process effectiveness, control findings |

---

## 7. Data Architecture

### 7.1 Data Sources

| Data Domain | Source System | Data Elements | Quality Controls |
|-------------|---------------|---------------|------------------|
| Credit Risk | Loan Origination System, Credit Risk System | EAD, PD, LGD, ratings, collateral | Automated validation, reconciliation |
| Market Risk | Trading System, Risk System | Positions, VaR, sensitivities | Timeliness checks, limit monitoring |
| Operational Risk | Operational Risk System | Loss data, KRI, RCSA results | Data completeness checks |
| Liquidity Risk | ALM System, Treasury | Cash flows, funding profile, LCR/NSFR | Reconciliation with GL |
| Financial Data | General Ledger, Planning System | P&L, balance sheet, capital ratios | Automated feeds, reconciliations |
| Macro-Economic | External providers (e.g., Bloomberg, IMF) | GDP, rates, unemployment | Source verification |

### 7.2 Data Flow Diagram

```
[Source Systems] --> [Data Integration Layer] --> [Risk Data Warehouse] --> [ICAAP Engine] --> [Reporting]
     |                       |                           |                       |                |
     |-- Transactional      |-- ETL/ELT                |-- Historical data     |-- Capital      |-- Dashboards
     |   data               |-- Data quality checks     |-- Risk data mart      |   calculations  |-- Regulatory reports
     |-- Market data        |-- Business rules          |-- Scenario data       |-- Stress tests |-- Management reports
     |-- Reference data     |-- Data lineage            |-- Model outputs       |-- Scenario     |-- Board packs
```

### 7.3 Systems Architecture

| System | Function | Owner |
|--------|----------|-------|
| Risk Data Warehouse (e.g., IBM OpenPages, SAS) | Central repository for risk data | Risk Management |
| ALM System (e.g., QRM, Moody's) | Asset-liability management, liquidity | Treasury |
| Credit Risk System (e.g., Moody's, FICO) | PD/LGD/EAD models, portfolio analysis | Risk Management |
| Market Risk System (e.g., Murex, Bloomberg) | VaR, sensitivity, counterparty risk | Risk Management |
| Operational Risk System (e.g., GRC platform) | Loss data, RCSA, KRIs | Risk Management |
| Planning System (e.g., Anaplan, Hyperion) | Business planning, budgeting | Finance |
| Reporting System (e.g., Tableau, Power BI, Cognos) | Dashboards, regulatory reports | Finance / Risk |
| Document Management (e.g., SharePoint) | Policy documents, audit trails | Compliance |

---

## 8. Implementation Roadmap

### Phase 1: Foundation (Months 1-3)
- [ ] Establish ICAAP governance structure and committees
- [ ] Develop ICAAP policy and methodology documents
- [ ] Conduct initial risk identification workshop
- [ ] Set up data architecture and data quality framework

### Phase 2: Build (Months 4-8)
- [ ] Develop and calibrate risk quantification models
- [ ] Implement stress testing framework
- [ ] Build capital planning models
- [ ] Integrate ICAAP with business planning system
- [ ] Develop reporting dashboards

### Phase 3: Operationalize (Months 9-12)
- [ ] Run first full ICAAP cycle
- [ ] Conduct reverse stress testing
- [ ] Prepare first ICAAP report
- [ ] Board review and approval
- [ ] Regulatory submission

### Phase 4: Embed and Enhance (Months 13-18)
- [ ] Post-implementation review
- [ ] Internal audit of ICAAP
- [ ] Refine methodologies based on lessons learned
- [ ] Enhance automation and data quality
- [ ] Ongoing training and awareness

---

## 9. Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) for ICAAP Effectiveness

| KPI | Target | Measurement Frequency |
|-----|--------|----------------------|
| Capital Adequacy Ratio (CAR) | > Regulatory minimum + buffer | Monthly |
| ICAAP cycle completion | Within annual timeline | Annual |
| Stress test coverage | All material risks covered | Annual |
| Data quality score | > 95% completeness and accuracy | Quarterly |
| Model validation findings | < 5 high-priority findings | Annual |
| ICAAP audit rating | Satisfactory or better | Annual |
| Regulatory feedback | No material adverse findings | Post-SREP |

---

## 10. Appendices

### Appendix A: Glossary of Terms

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| ICAAP | Internal Capital Adequacy Assessment Process |
| SREP | Supervisory Review and Evaluation Process |
| RAF | Risk Appetite Framework |
| RAS | Risk Appetite Statement |
| ICT | Internal Capital Target |
| ORSA | Own Risk and Solvency Assessment (for insurers) |
| ECL | Expected Credit Loss |
| IRB | Internal Ratings-Based approach |
| SA | Standardized Approach |
| VaR | Value at Risk |
| LCR | Liquidity Coverage Ratio |
| NSFR | Net Stable Funding Ratio |

### Appendix B: Regulatory References

- Basel Committee on Banking Supervision: Basel II/III Framework (Pillar 2)
- European Banking Authority: EBA Guidelines on ICAAP (EBA/GL/2023/XX)
- PRA: Supervisory Statement SS31/15 - The Internal Capital Adequacy Assessment Process
- PRA: Supervisory Statement SS13/16 - The Internal Capital Adequacy Assessment Process for insurers
- CRD IV/V: Capital Requirements Directive (Articles 73-77)
- CRR: Capital Requirements Regulation

### Appendix C: Document Control

| Version | Date | Author | Changes |
|---------|------|--------|---------|
| 0.1 | 2025-04-17 | Risk Management | Initial draft |
| 1.0 | 2025-04-17 | Risk Management | Final version |

---

**END OF DOCUMENT**

content:
# Governance Roles & Approvals Framework

## Operational Risk Management – Scenario Assessment & Model Risk Overlay

---

## 1. Purpose and Scope

This document defines the governance structure, roles, responsibilities, sign-off workflows, and escalation procedures governing:
- Operational Risk Scenario Assessments
- Loss Data Collection and Validation
- Model Risk Overlay (MRO) processes

It applies to all business units, risk functions, and internal audit activities within the organization.

---

## 2. Three-Tier Committee Structure

### 2.1 Tier 1 – Executive Risk Committee (ERC)

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Chair** | Chief Risk Officer (CRO) |
| **Members** | CEO, CFO, Head of Operational Risk, Chief Audit Executive, General Counsel, Chief Compliance Officer |
| **Frequency** | Quarterly (or special sessions as needed) |
| **Quorum** | Chair + 50% of members |

**Responsibilities:**
- Approve the Operational Risk Framework and Scenario Analysis methodology
- Endorse top-level risk appetite statements and scenario stress testing results
- Ratify material risk decisions and approve capital allocation for risk mitigation
- Review and approve the Model Risk Overlay standards and tolerances
- Escalate critical risk exposures to the Board Risk Committee

---

### 2.2 Tier 2 – Operational Risk Management Committee (ORMC)

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Chair** | Head of Operational Risk |
| **Members** | Business Unit Heads, Head of Model Risk, Head of IT Risk, Head of Compliance, Head of Internal Audit (observer) |
| **Frequency** | Monthly |
| **Quorum** | Chair + 40% of members |

**Responsibilities:**
- Review and validate scenario assessment results and loss data analyses
- Approve model risk overlay scores and challenge assumptions
- Review top operational risk exposures and ensure adequate mitigation plans
- Sign off on quarterly loss data submissions and scenario calibration
- Recommend escalations to the Executive Risk Committee

---

### 2.3 Tier 3 – Operational Risk Working Group (ORWG)

| Attribute | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Chair** | Senior Manager, Operational Risk |
| **Members** | Risk Analysts, Business Unit Risk Champions, Data Stewards, Model Owners, IT Security Leads |
| **Frequency** | Weekly / Bi-weekly |
| **Quorum** | Chair + 3 members |

**Responsibilities:**
- Perform detailed scenario analysis, loss data collection, and validation
- Conduct model risk overlay assessments and document findings
- Identify emerging risks and propose scenario updates
- Prepare materials and recommendations for ORMC review
- Maintain the operational risk loss database and scenario library

---

## 3. Roles and Responsibilities

### 3.1 Board of Directors / Board Risk Committee
- **Ultimate oversight** of the Operational Risk Framework
- **Approves** risk appetite, tolerance limits, and scenario methodologies
- **Reviews** material risk exposures and capital adequacy results

### 3.2 Chief Risk Officer (CRO)
- **Owns** the Operational Risk Framework
- **Chairs** the Executive Risk Committee
- **Signs off** on all material risk decisions and scenario assessments
- **Escalates** to the Board Risk Committee as needed

### 3.3 Head of Operational Risk
- **Manages** the day-to-day operational risk program
- **Chairs** the Operational Risk Management Committee
- **Validates** scenario results, loss data integrity, and model risk overlays
- **Recommends** risk appetite limits and escalation triggers

### 3.4 Business Unit Heads
- **Accountable** for risks within their units
- **Provide** subject matter expertise for scenario assessments
- **Implement** mitigation actions and controls
- **Certify** accuracy of loss data submissions

### 3.5 Model Risk Owner
- **Responsible** for model risk documentation and validation
- **Conducts** model risk overlay assessments (quantitative and qualitative)
- **Reports** overlay scores and findings to ORMC

### 3.6 Data Steward (Loss Data)
- **Maintains** the loss data collection system
- **Validates** data quality, completeness, and timeliness
- **Flags** data anomalies and threshold breaches

### 3.7 Internal Audit
- **Independent** assurance provider
- **Reviews** governance effectiveness and compliance with policy
- **Reports** findings directly to the Audit Committee

---

## 4. Sign-Off Workflow

### 4.1 Scenario Assessment Sign-Off

```
┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐
│  ORWG        │────▶│  ORMC        │────▶│  ERC         │────▶│  Board Risk  │
│  (Prepare)   │     │  (Review &   │     │  (Approve)   │     │  Committee   │
│              │     │   Validate)  │     │              │     │  (Ratify)    │
└──────────────┘     └──────────────┘     └──────────────┘     └──────────────┘
       │                    │                     │                     │
       │  Sign-off:         │  Sign-off:           │  Sign-off:           │  Sign-off:
       │  ORWG Chair        │  ORMC Chair          │  CRO                 │  Board Chair
       │  (SME)             │  (Head of OpRisk)   │                     │
```

### 4.2 Loss Data Sign-Off

| Step | Action | Responsible | Expected TAT |
|------|--------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | Report loss event (≥$1K threshold) | Business Unit | Within 24 hours |
| 2 | Validate and classify loss data | Data Steward | Within 5 business days |
| 3 | Review aggregated loss data | ORWG | Within 10 business days |
| 4 | Approve quarterly loss data submission | ORMC | Within 15 business days |
| 5 | Certify for regulatory reporting | CRO | Quarterly |

### 4.3 Model Risk Overlay Sign-Off

| Step | Action | Responsible | TAT |
|------|--------|-------------|-----|
| 1 | Conduct MRO assessment | Model Risk Owner | Per model cycle |
| 2 | Peer review MRO results | Senior Model Risk Analyst | Within 5 days |
| 3 | Challenge and validate overlay | ORWG | Within 10 days |
| 4 | Approve overlay scores | ORMC | Within 15 days |
| 5 | Endorse final MRO for capital computation | CRO | Quarterly |

---

## 5. Escalation Procedures

### 5.1 Escalation Triggers

| Trigger | Threshold | Escalate To | Timeframe |
|---------|-----------|-------------|-----------|
| **Single loss event** | ≥ $100K | Head of OpRisk | Immediate |
| **Single loss event** | ≥ $500K | CRO | Immediate |
| **Single loss event** | ≥ $1M | Board Risk Committee | < 24 hours |
| **Scenario result breach** | Exceeds risk appetite | ERC | Within 48 hours |
| **Model risk overlay score** | ≥ 4 (High) | CRO | Within 24 hours |
| **Model risk overlay score** | ≥ 5 (Critical) | Board Risk Committee | Immediate |
| **Data quality issue** | > 5% error rate | ORMC | Within 5 days |
| **Control failure** | Material weakness identified | ERC | Within 48 hours |

### 5.2 Escalation Path

```
Level 1: ORWG ──▶ ORMC ──▶ ERC ──▶ Board Risk Committee ──▶ Full Board
           │        │        │        │
           ▼        ▼        ▼        ▼
        (Issue)  (Trend)  (Material) (Critical)
```

### 5.3 Escalation Communication Protocol

1. **Initial Alert**: Verbal or email notification to the next level within defined timeframe
2. **Written Report**: Formal escalation memo within 24 hours (or 48 hours for non-critical)
3. **Root Cause Analysis**: Within 10 business days for material events
4. **Remediation Plan**: Proposed within 15 business days, tracked until closure

---

## 6. Model Risk Overlay – Governance Standards

### 6.1 Overlay Scoring Framework

| Score | Level | Description | Sign-Off Required |
|-------|-------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | Low | Model has minimal risk; well-validated | ORWG Chair |
| 2 | Moderate | Model has some limitations | ORMC Chair |
| 3 | Elevated | Model has material weaknesses | CRO |
| 4 | High | Model has significant deficiencies | Board Risk Committee |
| 5 | Critical | Model is unreliable for intended use | Full Board |

### 6.2 Overlay Review Frequency

| Overlay Score | Minimum Review Frequency |
|---------------|------------------------|
| 1 (Low) | Annually |
| 2 (Moderate) | Semi-annually |
| 3 (Elevated) | Quarterly |
| 4 (High) | Monthly |
| 5 (Critical) | Weekly (or suspend use) |

---

## 7. Delegation of Authority (DOA) Matrix

| Decision / Action | ORWG Chair | ORMC Chair | CRO | Board Risk Committee | Full Board |
|-------------------|:----------:|:----------:|:---:|:-------------------:|:----------:|
| Approve scenario methodology | - | - | ✓ | ✓ | Final |
| Approve scenario results (standard) | - | ✓ | ✓ | - | - |
| Approve scenario results (material) | - | - | ✓ | ✓ | - |
| Approve loss data threshold changes | - | ✓ | ✓ | - | - |
| Certify regulatory loss data | - | - | ✓ | - | - |
| Approve model risk overlay (score 1-2) | ✓ | - | - | - | - |
| Approve model risk overlay (score 3) | - | ✓ | ✓ | - | - |
| Approve model risk overlay (score 4) | - | - | ✓ | ✓ | - |
| Approve model risk overlay (score 5) | - | - | - | ✓ | ✓ |
| Escalate to regulator | - | - | ✓ | ✓ | Final |
| Approve risk appetite changes | - | - | - | ✓ | Final |
| Approve capital allocation | - | - | ✓ | ✓ | Final |

---

## 8. Meeting Cadence and Reporting

| Forum | Cadence | Key Outputs |
|-------|---------|-------------|
| ORWG | Weekly | Scenario working papers, loss data logs, MRO issue tracker |
| ORMC | Monthly | Scenario assessment summaries, loss data dashboards, MRO register |
| ERC | Quarterly | Risk appetite dashboard, capital results, top risk exposures |
| Board Risk Committee | Quarterly | Strategic risk overview, material incidents, capital adequacy |
| Full Board | Semi-annually | Risk framework confirmation, emerging risk themes |

---

## 9. Documentation and Records

All governance activities must produce documented evidence:

| Document | Owner | Retention |
|----------|-------|-----------|
| Meeting minutes and attendance | Committee Secretary | 7 years |
| Sign-off records (digital/paper) | Head of OpRisk | 7 years |
| Escalation memos | CRO | 10 years |
| Scenario assessment working papers | ORWG Chair | 5 years |
| Loss data certification | CRO | 10 years |
| Model risk overlay reports | Model Risk Owner | 7 years |

---

## 10. Policy Exceptions

Any deviation from this governance framework must be:
1. Documented in a formal Exception Request
2. Approved by the CRO (or Board Risk Committee for material exceptions)
3. Tracked in the Exception Register
4. Reviewed at least quarterly for remediation progress

---

## 11. Version Control and Review

| Version | Date | Author | Changes |
|---------|------|--------|---------|
| 1.0 | [Current Date] | Operational Risk Team | Initial release |

**Next Review Date:** [Current Date + 12 months] or upon material change to risk profile.

---

*This framework aligns with Basel Committee principles, ISO 31000:2018, and COSO ERM 2017 standards.*

name: ICAAP Risk Appetite & Limits Framework
description: ICAAP Risk Appetite and Limits Framework document covering the risk appetite statement, hierarchical limit system across three levels, and a tiered breach escalation process.
content:
---
ICAAP Risk Appetite & Limits Framework
---

1. Risk Appetite Statement
The Bank maintains a moderate risk appetite. We seek stable returns while keeping capital well above regulatory minima. We will not take risks that could threaten solvency or breach regulatory thresholds under stressed conditions.

2. Hierarchical Limit System

Level 1 – Board-Level Appetite (Overall)
- Capital Adequacy Ratio: >= 12.5%
- Leverage Ratio: >= 4.5%
- Maximum single-name concentration: <= 20% of Tier 1 capital

Level 2 – Management-Level Limits (By Risk Type)
- Credit Risk: NPL ratio <= 5.0%
- Market Risk: Value at Risk (99% 1-day) <= 0.5% of capital
- Liquidity Risk: LCR >= 120%, NSFR >= 105%
- Operational Risk: Losses <= 0.3% of gross income

Level 3 – Operational/Tactical Limits (By Business Unit)
- Business units have sub-limits allocated by ALCO.
- Traders have position limits relative to daily P&L.

3. Breach Escalation Process

Tier 1 (Minor / Within 5% of limit):
- Business unit head notified within 1 business day.
- Remediation plan required within 5 business days.

Tier 2 (Significant / 5-15% above limit):
- CRO and Risk Committee notified within 24 hours.
- Immediate remediation action required.
- Risk Committee reviews at next scheduled meeting.

Tier 3 (Critical / >15% above limit):
- Board notified immediately.
- All relevant exposures frozen where possible.
- Emergency Board meeting called within 48 hours.
- Capital contingency plan activated if solvency threatened.

4. Review Cycle
- Risk appetite statement reviewed annually by the Board.
- Limits reviewed quarterly by the Risk Committee.
- Breach events logged and reported to regulators as required.

description: Risk inventory table covering seven risk types with gross materiality ratings, methods, and owners
content:
# ICAAP Risk Inventory & Materiality Assessment

## 1. Risk Inventory Overview

| Risk Type | Sub-Categories | Gross Materiality | Assessment Method | Risk Owner |
|-----------|----------------|-------------------|-------------------|------------|
| Credit Risk | On-balance sheet, off-balance sheet, counterparty | High | Standardised Approach (SA) / IRB | CRO |
| Market Risk | Trading book, FX, commodities | Medium | VaR / Stressed VaR | Head of Market Risk |
| CVA Risk | Derivative counterparty credit valuation adjustments | Medium | CVA VaR / Standardised CVA | Head of Market Risk |
| IRRBB | Net interest income sensitivity, EVE sensitivity | Medium | Earnings at Risk / EVE Simulation | Treasurer / ALM |
| Operational Risk | Internal fraud, external fraud, systems, processes | High | Basic Indicator / Standardised Approach | Head of Operational Risk |
| Concentration Risk | Single-name, sectoral, geographic | High | Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI), limits framework | CRO |
| Model Risk | Model development, validation, implementation | Medium | Model risk tiering, independent validation | Head of Model Validation |

## 2. Materiality Assessment (Gross Approach)

Gross materiality is assessed on a pre-mitigation basis (i.e., before considering risk controls or mitigants). The approach considers:
- **Scale**: Potential loss magnitude relative to capital and earnings
- **Frequency**: Likelihood of occurrence based on historical data and forward-looking indicators
- **Complexity**: Degree of difficulty in measuring and managing the risk

| Materiality Level | Definition |
|-------------------|------------|
| High | Could cause material impairment to capital or earnings; requires dedicated capital allocation and detailed management |
| Medium | Notable but less severe; monitored via limits and standard capital charges |
| Low | Immaterial in normal conditions; covered under general buffers |

## 3. Methodology Mapping

| Risk Type | Key Methods Used |
|-----------|------------------|
| Credit Risk | PD/LGD/EAD models, stress testing, portfolio analysis |
| Market Risk | VaR (historical/Monte Carlo), sensitivity analysis, stress scenarios |
| CVA | Simulation of credit spreads and exposure profiles |
| IRRBB | Gap analysis, duration analysis, dynamic simulation |
| Operational Risk | Loss data collection, scenario analysis, RCSA |
| Concentration Risk | Portfolio concentration metrics, stress testing of correlated exposures |
| Model Risk | Validation reports, back-testing, benchmarking |

## 4. Owner Accountability

Each risk type has a designated risk owner responsible for:
- Maintaining the risk inventory and methodology
- Reporting materiality changes to the Risk Committee
- Ensuring capital adequacy assessments reflect the risk profile

The CRO has overall accountability for the ICAAP framework.

name: Completed: Segment LGD Estimation and Validation
description:
AI agent completed task: Executive Summary: Estimate loss given default (LGD) for each segment using recovery and exposure data, then validate against historical recovery rates and stress scenarios.

Implementation Plan: 1. Extract workout LGD and cure rates from prepared data. 2. For each segment, fit a beta regression or Tobit model to capture LGD bounded between 0 and 1. 3. Benchmark against downturn LGD and long-run averages. 4. Perform backtesting using realised LGD from recent defaults. 5. Document model form, parameter stability, and validation metrics.

Acceptance Criteria: LGD models for all segments are fitted with mean absolute error < 10% vs. historical averages; backtesting shows no systematic bias; all assumptions documented.

name: ead_estimation_validation_report.md
description: Human-readable Markdown report summarizing all EAD estimation, CCF analysis, validation results, segment mapping, and regulatory floor documentation.
content:
# Segment EAD Estimation and Validation Report

## Executive Summary

This report documents the estimation of Exposure at Default (EAD) across four key segments: Retail Revolving (Credit Cards), Corporate Revolving Facilities, Term Loans (Amortizing), and Off-Balance-Sheet Instruments. Credit Conversion Factors (CCFs) were estimated using both individual dollar-CCF and ratio-of-means methods. Validation against actual EAD realisations shows all segments pass the <15% deviation threshold, with overall deviation of 4.77%.

---

## 1. Segmentation Overview

| Segment ID | Segment Name | PD Segment | LGD Segment | Method |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SEG-01 | Retail Revolving (Credit Cards) | Retail Low Risk | Unsecured Retail | CCF (Undrawn * CCF) |
| SEG-02 | Corporate Revolving Facilities | Corporate Standard | Senior Unsecured Corporate | CCF (Undrawn * CCF) |
| SEG-03 | Term Loans (Amortizing) | Corporate Standard | Senior Secured Corporate | Current Exposure + PFE |
| SEG-04 | Off-Balance-Sheet (Guarantees, LCs) | Corporate Standard | Senior Unsecured Corporate | CCF (Notional * CCF) |

---

## 2. CCF Estimation Results

### 2.1 Retail Revolving (Credit Cards) — SEG-01
- **Sample Size:** 2,000 accounts
- **Dollar CCF (Average):** 0.752
- **Ratio-of-Means CCF:** 0.748
- **Final CCF:** 0.750 (Regulatory floor: 50% — not binding)
- **CCF Distribution:** Mean=0.750, Median=0.760, Std=0.150, P5=0.480, P95=0.980
- **Estimated EAD:** 7,200,000
- **Actual EAD:** 7,100,000
- **Deviation:** 1.41% ✅ (Passes < 15%)

### 2.2 Corporate Revolving Facilities — SEG-02
- **Sample Size:** 500 accounts
- **Dollar CCF (Average):** 0.452
- **Ratio-of-Means CCF:** 0.442
- **Final CCF:** 0.447 (No regulatory floor applied)
- **CCF Distribution:** Mean=0.447, Median=0.410, Std=0.220, P5=0.150, P95=0.850
- **Estimated EAD:** 104,055,000
- **Actual EAD:** 98,500,000
- **Deviation:** 5.64% ✅ (Passes < 15%)

### 2.3 Term Loans (Amortizing) — SEG-03
- **Sample Size:** 300 accounts
- **Method:** Current Exposure + PFE Adjustment (5%)
- **Current Exposure:** 120,000,000
- **PFE Adjustment:** 6,000,000
- **Estimated EAD:** 126,000,000
- **Actual EAD:** 123,000,000
- **Deviation:** 2.44% ✅ (Passes < 15%)

### 2.4 Off-Balance-Sheet (Guarantees, LCs) — SEG-04
- **Sample Size:** 400 instruments
- **Dollar CCF (Average):** 0.623
- **Ratio-of-Means CCF:** 0.615
- **Final CCF:** 0.619 (Regulatory floor: 50% — not binding)
- **CCF Distribution:** Mean=0.619, Median=0.640, Std=0.180, P5=0.310, P95=0.920
- **Estimated EAD:** 30,950,000
- **Actual EAD:** 29,500,000
- **Deviation:** 4.92% ✅ (Passes < 15%)

---

## 3. Validation Summary

| Segment | Estimated EAD | Actual EAD | Deviation (%) | Pass/Fail |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Retail Revolving | 7,200,000 | 7,100,000 | 1.41% | ✅ Pass |
| Corporate Revolving | 104,055,000 | 98,500,000 | 5.64% | ✅ Pass |
| Term Loans | 126,000,000 | 123,000,000 | 2.44% | ✅ Pass |
| Off-Balance-Sheet | 30,950,000 | 29,500,000 | 4.92% | ✅ Pass |
| **Overall** | **268,055,000** | **255,850,000** | **4.77%** | **✅ All Pass** |

---

## 4. Segment Mapping Documentation

Each EAD segment is explicitly mapped to corresponding PD and LGD segments to ensure consistency across the risk parameter framework:

| EAD Segment | PD Segment | LGD Segment | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| Retail Revolving (Credit Cards) | Retail Low Risk | Unsecured Retail | Standard retail segmentation; revolving nature requires CCF approach |
| Corporate Revolving Facilities | Corporate Standard | Senior Unsecured Corporate | Corporate revolving; typically unsecured or partially secured |
| Term Loans (Amortizing) | Corporate Standard | Senior Secured Corporate | Fully drawn amortizing; secured by collateral |
| Off-Balance-Sheet (Guarantees, LCs) | Corporate Standard | Senior Unsecured Corporate | Contingent obligations; CCF applied to notional |

---

## 5. Regulatory Floors

- **Retail Revolving:** Basel IRB floor of 50% for undrawn revolving retail commitments. Applied: 50% floor. Final CCF (0.750) exceeds floor; not binding.
- **Corporate Revolving:** No explicit regulatory floor under IRB; final CCF (0.447) used as-is.
- **Term Loans:** N/A — not a CCF-based exposure.
- **Off-Balance-Sheet:** Basel IRB floor of 50% for certain OBS items (guarantees, LCs). Applied: 50% floor. Final CCF (0.619) exceeds floor; not binding.

---

## 6. Methodology

### CCF Estimation Methods

1. **Dollar CCF (Individual):** \( \text{CCF}_i = \frac{\text{EAD}_i - \text{Drawn}_i}{\text{Undrawn}_i} \)
   - Calculated per account; extreme values winsorized at 0 and 1.
   - Averaged across the segment.

2. **Ratio of Means (Aggregate):** \( \text{CCF} = \frac{\sum(\text{EAD}_i - \text{Drawn}_i)}{\sum(\text{Undrawn}_i)} \)
   - Portfolio-level approach; robust to outliers.

3. **Final CCF:** Average of the two methods above, floored at regulatory minimum.

### Validation Method

\[ \text{Deviation} = \frac{|\text{Estimated EAD} - \text{Actual EAD}|}{\text{Actual EAD}} \times 100\% \]

**Acceptance Criterion:** Deviation < 15% for each segment.

---

## 7. Conclusions

- All four segments pass validation with deviations well under the 15% threshold.
- CCF estimates are stable across estimation methods (dollar CCF vs. ratio-of-means differ by <0.01 in all cases).
- Regulatory floors were applied where required but are not binding for current estimates.
- Segment mapping is fully documented and aligned with PD/LGD segments.
- Recommended periodic (quarterly) re-estimation to monitor CCF stability over time.

name: reverse_stress_test_framework_v1.0.md
description: Comprehensive Reverse Stress Test Framework covering non-viability conditions (capital, liquidity, funding, franchise), five distinct pathways (sovereign, credit, market, operational, contagion), quantitative parameterization tables, and a four-level remediation escalation ladder with governance and monitoring provisions.
content:
================================================================================
                 REVERSE STRESS TEST FRAMEWORK
                 Institution-Wide Non-Viability Analysis
================================================================================

DOCUMENT TYPE: Reverse Stress Test Framework & Parameterization
VERSION: 1.0

================================================================================
1. DEFINITION OF NON-VIABILITY CONDITIONS
================================================================================

Non-viability is defined as any state in which the institution can no longer
operate as a going concern without extraordinary external support (e.g., central
bank emergency lending, government bailout, or resolution regime entry).

--- 1.1 Quantitative Non-Viability Thresholds ---

  Condition A — Capital Exhaustion
    • Common Equity Tier 1 (CET1) ratio falls below 1.5%
    • Total Capital ratio falls below 4.0%
    • Tier 1 Leverage ratio falls below 1.0%
    Rationale: Below these levels, the institution cannot absorb further losses
    and regulatory mandatory triggers (PONV, minimum requirements) are breached.

  Condition B — Liquidity Insolvency
    • Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR) falls below 50% for 5+ consecutive days
    • Net Stable Funding Ratio (NSFR) falls below 60% for 2+ consecutive weeks
    • Intraday liquidity position breaches minimum operating balance
    Rationale: The institution cannot meet payment obligations as they fall due,
    leading to default on secured/unsecured obligations and potential market
    infrastructure suspension.

  Condition C — Funding Market Access Loss
    • Wholesale unsecured funding markets become completely inaccessible
      (zero new issuance capacity for 10+ consecutive business days)
    • Secured funding (repo) haircuts increase >50% on previously eligible
      collateral pools
    • Credit rating downgrade to below investment grade (or 3-notch downgrade
      within 30 days) triggering collateral calls that cannot be met
    Rationale: Loss of market confidence creates a self-reinforcing downward
    spiral from which the institution cannot recover without official sector
    intervention.

  Condition D — Counterparty & Franchise Collapse
    • Loss of 2+ of top 5 clearing memberships or settlement access
    • 5+ consecutive days of CDS spreads >1,500 bps
    • Material litigation/regulatory fines exceeding 50% of Tier 1 capital
    • Key person or operational capability loss preventing critical functions
    Rationale: The institution's franchise is destroyed; clients, counterparties,
    and employees cease to transact with or work for the institution.

--- 1.2 Qualitative Non-Viability Indicators (Early-Warning Gate) ---

  • External auditor issues going-concern qualification
  • Regulatory breach notification to home/host supervisors
  • Activation of recovery plan's most severe indicators (red zone)
  • Board determines in its judgment that viability is at risk

================================================================================
2. NON-VIABILITY PATHWAYS & STORYLINES
================================================================================

Each pathway describes a plausible chain of events that, if unchecked,
results in one or more of the non-viability conditions above.

--- PATHWAY A — Sovereign & Macroeconomic Tail Risk ---
  Trigger Event: Major sovereign debt restructuring in a key exposure
  country (e.g., EU periphery, large emerging market).
  Transmission:
    • Sovereign downgrades → collateral value destruction → margin calls
    • Bank holds significant sovereign exposure → mark-to-market losses
    • Wider credit spreads → counterparty credit risk revaluation
    • Deposit flight from affected region → liquidity drain
  Non-Viability Link: Conditions A (capital) and B (liquidity) reached
  simultaneously due to concentrated sovereign exposure.

--- PATHWAY B — Credit Cycle & Concentration Failure ---
  Trigger Event: Synchronized default of top 5 single-name exposures
  (e.g., commercial real estate developers in a property crash).
  Transmission:
    • NPL ratio spikes from ~2% to >15%
    • Large impairment charges deplete CET1
    • Rating downgrade → collateral calls on derivatives
    • Loss of confidence from unsecured creditors → wholesale funding freeze
  Non-Viability Link: Condition A (capital) breached first; Condition C
  (funding access) follows, creating a compound failure.

--- PATHWAY C — Market Dislocation & Trading Book Loss ---
  Trigger Event: Flash crash or liquidity black hole in a core market
  (e.g., interest rate derivatives, FX, or equity markets).
  Transmission:
    • Large directional position cannot be unwound without moving market
    • VaR breaches → limits escalated → forced de-risking at distressed prices
    • Model risk: period of extreme volatility invalidates risk models
    • Collateral disputes → margin calls exceed available liquidity
  Non-Viability Link: Condition C (funding) triggered by margin calls;
  Conditions A (capital) and D (franchise) follow as counterparties
  reduce lines.

--- PATHWAY D — Operational & Conduct Catastrophe ---
  Trigger Event: Major operational risk event (IT system failure,
  cyberattack, unauthorized trading, or regulatory sanction).
  Transmission:
    • System outage → settlement failures → fines and penalties
    • Reputational damage → client outflows (retail deposits + AUM)
    • Regulatory sanction includes business restriction (e.g., suspension
      of license in material jurisdiction)
    • Litigation costs + redress payments erode capital
  Non-Viability Link: Condition D (franchise) breached first; Conditions
  B and A follow as the franchise destruction accelerates.

--- PATHWAY E — Interconnectedness & Systemic Contagion ---
  Trigger Event: Failure of a major counterparty or peer institution
  (e.g., G-SIB default or near-default).
  Transmission:
    • Direct exposure (credit, derivatives, settlement) crystallizes
    • Indirect exposure via market-wide loss of confidence
    • Interbank market freezes; even secured lending ceases
    • Fire sales depress asset prices → further losses
  Non-Viability Link: All four conditions hit near-simultaneously;
  systemic stress means no external support available.

================================================================================
3. PARAMETERIZATION OF SCENARIOS
================================================================================

--- 3.1 Macro-Financial Shock Parameters ---

  Parameter               | Baseline   | Adverse    | Severe       | Extreme
  -------------------------|------------|------------|--------------|------------
  GDP (cumulative change) | 0%         | -4%        | -8%          | -15%
  Unemployment rate       | 5%         | 8%         | 12%          | 18%
  Equity market decline   | 0%         | -25%       | -45%         | -65%
  Property prices (CRE)   | 0%         | -20%       | -40%         | -55%
  Property prices (RES)   | 0%         | -15%       | -25%         | -35%
  Interest rates (bps chg)| 0          | +100       | +200         | +400
  Credit spreads (bps)    | +10        | +150       | +350         | +750
  FX (EM depreciation)    | 0%         | -15%       | -30%         | -50%
  Sovereign CDS (bps)     | +5         | +100       | +300         | +600
  Inflation rate          | 2%         | 4%         | 7%           | 12%

--- 3.2 Specific Reverse Stress Parameters (Pathway-Specific) ---

  Pathway A – Sovereign Tail:
    • Sovereign exposure loss rate: 25-60% (depending on jurisdiction)
    • Haircut on sovereign collateral: 40-80%
    • Deposit run rate: 10-25% of affected jurisdiction deposits per week
    • Cross-border funding freeze: 3-6 months duration

  Pathway B – Credit Concentration:
    • PD on top 5 exposures: 60-100%
    • LGD on defaulted exposures: 60-80%
    • Correlation factor across exposures: 0.7-0.9 (concentration)
    • Recovery rate on risk-weighted assets: 10-20%

  Pathway C – Market Dislocation:
    • Volatility multiplier vs. historical: 5x-10x
    • Market liquidity discount (bid-ask spread): 10-50x normal
    • Correlation breakdown: asset class correlations shift from 0.3 to -0.5
    • Margin call size: 20-40% of derivative notional per day

  Pathway D – Operational:
    • System downtime: 5-30 business days
    • Direct loss (fraud/unauthorized): 5-15% of Tier 1 capital
    • Regulatory fine: 10-25% of Tier 1 capital
    • Franchise outflow (deposits/AUM): 15-40% over 3 months

  Pathway E – Contagion:
    • Counterparty default loss: 20-50% of exposure
    • Market-wide haircut increase: 30-60% on all collateral
    • Interbank lending freeze duration: 2-8 weeks
    • Systemic risk premium (CDS): +500 to +1,500 bps

--- 3.3 Time Horizon Assumptions ---

  • Capital erosion period: 1-6 quarters
  • Liquidity stress period: 2-12 weeks
  • Funding market shut-out: up to 6 months
  • Full scenario duration (incl. recovery): 2-3 years
  • Window for remediation action before non-viability: 1-8 weeks
    (varies by pathway; Pathway C (market) is fastest, Pathway B (credit)
    is slowest)

================================================================================
4. REMEDIATION ACTIONS & ESCALATION LADDER
================================================================================

--- 4.1 Pre-Stress / Readiness Actions (Normal Times) ---
  • Maintain capital buffers above regulatory minima (P2G + CCB + systemic)
  • Hold high-quality liquid asset (HQLA) buffer > 110% of LCR requirement
  • Diversify funding sources: maintain access to at least 3 distinct
    unsecured funding markets
  • Pre-position collateral at central bank and CCPs
  • Limit single-name concentration to < 15% of CET1
  • Conduct regular reverse stress test trainings and simulations

--- 4.2 Early Remediation (Amber/Yellow Zone — 1st Non-Viability Gate) ---
  Triggers:
    • CET1 ratio breaches 5.5% (recovery plan trigger)
    • LCR falls below 80% (internal trigger)
    • Rating agency places institution on negative watch
    • Any qualitative non-viability indicator activated

  Actions:
    [Capital]
      • Cancel dividend and share buyback programs
      • Reduce discretionary bonus pool
      • Halt discretionary risk-taking (above-average risk assets)
      • Initiate contingent convertible (CoCo) coupon cancellation

    [Liquidity & Funding]
      • Activate contingency funding plan (CFP) — Level 1
      • Draw down committed credit lines
      • Lengthen term of new funding issuance
      • Collateral upgrade program (swap lower-quality for HQLA)

    [Business]
      • Reduce risk-weighted assets (RWA) via natural runoff
      • Stop new lending in most-stressed sectors
      • Review and tighten credit limits for top exposures

--- 4.3 Intensive Remediation (Red Zone — 2nd Non-Viability Gate) ---
  Triggers:
    • CET1 ratio breaches 4.0%
    • LCR falls below 70%
    • First credit rating downgrade below investment grade
    • Wholesale funding markets show clear signs of restricted access

  Actions:
    [Capital]
      • Equity raising (rights issue/private placement) — target: 2-3% CET1
      • Asset sales (non-core portfolios, subsidiaries)
      • Liability management exercise (debt buyback/swap)
      • AT1/T2 coupon suspension

    [Liquidity & Funding]
      • CFP — Level 2 (full activation)
      • Access central bank standing facilities (discount window, MLA)
      • Securitization of assets for secured funding
      • Renegotiate credit lines and extend maturities
      • Targeted deposit rate increases to retain retail deposits

    [Business]
      • Divest non-core business units
      • Close or significantly reduce trading books
      • Halt all non-essential lending
      • Repo-to-maturity operations to lock in funding

--- 4.4 Recovery-to-Resolution Bridge (Critical Zone — Pre-Non-Viability) ---
  Triggers:
    • CET1 ratio breaches 2.5%
    • LCR falls below 50%
    • Access to all unsecured markets lost
    • Board determines non-viability is imminent within 2 weeks

  Actions:
    [Capital]
      • Full bail-in of all regulatory capital instruments
      • Convert debt to equity under bail-in framework
      • Apply for government capital injection / asset protection scheme
      • Prepare resolution pack (FMU notification, living document update)

    [Liquidity & Funding]
      • CFP — Level 3 (emergency)
      • Emergency Liquidity Assistance (ELA) from central bank
      • Collateralize all remaining unencumbered assets
      • Suspension of non-critical payment obligations (legal review)

    [Business]
      • Wind-down of remaining trading books
      • Transfer critical functions to bridge bank (resolution plan)
      • Cease all new business origination
      • Notify all major counterparties of resolution plan activation

--- 4.5 Monitoring & Governance ---

  Frequency of stress test runs:
    • Full reverse stress test: at least annually
    • Pathway-specific reverse stress tests: semi-annually
    • Ad-hoc reverse stress test: upon material change in risk profile,
      business model, or macroeconomic environment

  Governance:
    • Board Risk Committee: reviews and approves framework annually
    • Executive Risk Committee: reviews results, approves remediation actions
    • Chief Risk Officer: signs off on methodology and assumptions
    • Internal Audit: reviews process every 2 years
    • Results submitted to regulator as part of ICAAP/ILAAP

  Validation:
    • Model validation team reviews parameterization and assumptions
    • Independent challenge from internal audit or external consultant
    • Historical benchmarking: compare to past crisis episodes (2008, 2011,
      2020, 2023)
    • Sensitivity analysis on key parameters (one-at-a-time and multivariate)

================================================================================
5. DOCUMENTATION AND OUTPUT REQUIREMENTS
================================================================================

  For each reverse stress test run, the following must be documented:
    1. The non-viability condition(s) targeted
    2. The pathway narrative and logical chain of events
    3. Parameter values and assumptions (with justification)
    4. Losses incurred (by risk type and business line)
    5. Post-stress capital and liquidity positions
    6. Point at which non-viability is reached (time + trigger)
    7. Remediation actions taken and their impact (with and without)
    8. Sensitivity analysis on key assumptions
    9. Management commentary and proposed actions
   10. Board sign-off

================================================================================
               END OF REVERSE STRESS TEST FRAMEWORK
================================================================================

================================================================================
           REVERSE STRESS TEST — PARAMETER WORKBOOK
           Quantitative Shocks, Thresholds & Calibration
================================================================================

SHEET 1: NON-VIABILITY THRESHOLDS (Input Table)

Metric                          | Threshold          | Data Source
---------------------------------|--------------------|----------------------------
CET1 Ratio                      | < 1.5%             | Regulatory returns (COREP)
Total Capital Ratio             | < 4.0%             | Regulatory returns (COREP)
Tier 1 Leverage Ratio           | < 1.0%             | Regulatory returns (COREP)
LCR                             | < 50% x 5 days     | Internal liquidity report
NSFR                            | < 60% x 2 weeks    | Internal liquidity report
Wholesale funding access        | Zero for 10+ days  | Funding desk report
CDS spread                      | > 1,500 bps x 5d   | Market data (Bloomberg)
Top client loss (deposits/AUM)  | > 25% in 1 month   | Business line reports

SHEET 2: PATHWAY PARAMETERS (Scenario Design)

PATHWAY A — Sovereign Tail:
  Parameter                          | Adverse    | Severe     | Extreme
  -----------------------------------|------------|------------|------------
  Sovereign exposure loss rate       | 25%        | 40%        | 60%
  Sov. collateral haircut            | 40%        | 60%        | 80%
  Deposit run rate (weekly)          | 10%        | 18%        | 25%
  Funding freeze duration (months)   | 3          | 4          | 6

PATHWAY B — Credit Concentration:
  Parameter                          | Adverse    | Severe     | Extreme
  -----------------------------------|------------|------------|------------
  PD on top 5 exposures              | 60%        | 80%        | 100%
  LGD on defaulted exposures         | 60%        | 70%        | 80%
  Cross-exposure correlation         | 0.7        | 0.8        | 0.9
  RWA recovery rate                  | 20%        | 15%        | 10%

PATHWAY C — Market Dislocation:
  Parameter                          | Adverse    | Severe     | Extreme
  -----------------------------------|------------|------------|------------
  Volatility multiplier (x hist)     | 5x         | 7x         | 10x
  Bid-ask spread multiplier (x norm) | 10x        | 25x        | 50x
  Correlation shift (asset classes)  | 0.3→0.0    | 0.3→-0.3   | 0.3→-0.5
  Daily margin call (% of notional)  | 20%        | 30%        | 40%

PATHWAY D — Operational Catastrophe:
  Parameter                          | Adverse    | Severe     | Extreme
  -----------------------------------|------------|------------|------------
  System downtime (business days)    | 5          | 15         | 30
  Direct loss (% of Tier 1 capital)  | 5%         | 10%        | 15%
  Regulatory fine (% Tier 1 capital) | 10%        | 18%        | 25%
  Franchise outflow (deposits/AUM)   | 15%        | 28%        | 40%

PATHWAY E — Contagion:
  Parameter                          | Adverse    | Severe     | Extreme
  -----------------------------------|------------|------------|------------
  Counterparty loss (% of exposure)  | 20%        | 35%        | 50%
  Collateral haircut increase        | 30%        | 45%        | 60%
  Interbank freeze duration (weeks)  | 2          | 5          | 8
  Systemic CDS premium (bps)         | 500        | 1,000      | 1,500

SHEET 3: REMEDIATION TIMING & EFFECTIVENESS

Action                             | Time to Execute | Effectiveness | Dependencies
------------------------------------|-----------------|---------------|---------------------
Dividend cancellation              | 1-2 days        | High (saves 1-3% CET1) | Board approval
RWA reduction (runoff)             | 2-4 weeks       | Medium        | Market conditions
Asset sales                        | 4-12 weeks      | High          | Buyer availability
Equity raising (rights)            | 4-8 weeks       | Very High     | Market conditions
CFP - Level 1 activation           | Same day        | High          | Pre-positioned HQLA
CFP - Level 2 activation           | 1-2 days        | High          | Collateral availability
ELA access                         | 2-5 days        | Very High     | Central bank eligibility
Business divestiture               | 8-24 weeks      | Medium-High   | Regulatory approval
CoCo coupon cancellation           | 1-2 days        | Low-Medium    | Contract terms

================================================================================
               END OF PARAMETER WORKBOOK
================================================================================
